interactions.summary: Jiang finishes the bully parable and applies it to the U.S. escalation ladder. The bully’s need to preserve face makes him manipulable; the United States likewise moves through decapitation, military strikes, embargo, civilian infrastructure attacks, secret weapons, biochemical weapons, and only much later nuclear weapons.

speaker_notes.note: Diarization is a hint; focus refs read as Jiang lecture except where student question spans are explicitly separated.

claims.claim: The new kid creates rebellion because other students and even the bully’s allies begin to see alternative alliances once someone refuses the cafeteria rules.
The bully has dominance but loses control because his credibility depends on demonstrating power; calibrated resistance can force him into retreat or self-destruction.
Jiang maps the U.S. escalation ladder as decapitation, military targets, economic embargo, civilian infrastructure, secret weapons, biochemical weapons, then nuclear weapons.
claims.temporal_scope: Model of the U.S.-Iran war stated on 2026-03-10.

claims.claim: He says U.S. decapitation and military-target attacks have not worked, and attacks on civilian water and oil infrastructure mark the current stage of escalation.
claims.temporal_scope: Assessment of current war stage on 2026-03-10.

claims.claim: Jiang calls attacks on civilian infrastructure war crimes or violations of international law, while explaining them as the move actors make when they need more pressure to win.
He rejects near-term nuclear-use fears because, in his escalation model, secret and biochemical weapons have not appeared, so nuclear weapons are still several steps away.
Civilian-infrastructure attacks can backfire by uniting the population behind the government, when the attacker’s aim is to split people from the regime.
claims.temporal_scope: General model applied to Iran on 2026-03-10.

signature_moments.moment: The weak actor can calibrate the bully into destroying himself.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the bridge from a schoolyard story to Jiang’s claim that Iran can control a stronger enemy.

signature_moments.moment: Decapitation is explained brutally simply: kill the leaders so the body does not know what to do.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the U.S. first move a stark mechanical definition.

signature_moments.moment: Bombing civilians may strengthen the very government it was supposed to split from the people.
signature_moments.source_phrase: you unite the people behind the government
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This reversal is central to Jiang’s argument against blunt escalation.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A strategy of killing leaders so the opponent loses command and surrenders.
A later escalation step targeting water, oil, or other civilian systems to pressure surrender, which Jiang says risks unifying the population.
An escalation step involving advanced weapons meant to shock the opponent before biochemical or nuclear escalation.

chronology_notes.note: As of 2026-03-10 Jiang says nuclear weapons are not yet on the table because the war has not passed secret or biochemical weapon stages.
